message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Removing the Defender (eliminating the piece that guards a key square, piece or mating square)
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Capture or chase the defender, then execute the threat. Show capturing a knight that defends a back-rank mating square.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/removing-the-defender.md

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").

TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

input.content:
# Removing the Defender

> Eliminating the piece that guards a key square, piece, or mating square — so the real threat can land unopposed.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md), [Pins](pins.md), none beyond basic tactics

## The idea

Many threats fail for a single reason: one enemy piece is doing defensive work. It guards the mating square, holds a hanging piece together, or covers the only escape route. **Removing the Defender** (also called *removal of the guard* or *undermining*) is the technique of getting rid of that one piece so the threat becomes unstoppable.

There are two practical ways to do it:

1. **Capture the defender** — trade or sacrifice to take it off the board.
2. **Chase / deflect the defender** — force it to move away with a threat it must answer.

Once the guard is gone, you immediately execute the original threat: the mate, the winning capture, the promotion. The order matters — *remove first, strike second.*

## Why it matters

At the board you rarely get a clean, undefended target. Strong defense means every key square is covered. Recognizing **which single piece** holds the position together turns "I can't break through" into "I capture that knight and it's mate." It is the bridge between seeing a threat and actually delivering it.

This motif underlies a huge share of combinations: back-rank mates, smothered mates, won endgames where you eliminate the blockader of a passed pawn, and queen-side attacks where one defender covers two squares at once. Train it and your tactical vision roughly doubles, because you stop evaluating the *current* position and start evaluating the position *after the guard disappears*.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . R P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to play: the rook on f8 guards the back-rank entry square f8.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5RPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5RPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1

The black king is boxed in by its own pawns on f7-g7-h7. White wants Re8#, but f8 is covered by the black rook. So White **removes the defender**: **1.Rxf8+ Rxf8 2.Re8#** — after the trade the only piece guarding e8/f8 is gone, and the rook delivers mate along the back rank.

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . n . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to play: the knight on f6 is the only thing covering the mating square e8.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/5n2/8/8/8/6PP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/5n2/8/8/8/6PP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1

Here White has only one rook, and **1.Re8?? Rxe8** simply loses it because the f6-knight guards e8. The knight is the defender. White must capture it first — but a direct capture isn't available, so this is a *recognition* position: identify that the f6-knight is the guard. (See Worked Examples for the position where capturing it is possible.) The lesson: name the defender before you calculate.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Capture the guard** — take the defending piece, accepting the recapture, because the follow-up threat is bigger (mate or a larger material gain).
- **Chase the guard** — attack the defender with a pawn or minor piece so it must flee, abandoning its duty.
- **Deflection** — lure the defender to a square where it no longer guards the key point (closely related; see [[deflection]]).
- **Overloaded defender** — the piece guards two things; remove it and *both* collapse.
- **Removing the blockader** — in endgames, eliminate the piece sitting in front of a passed pawn so it promotes.
- **Undermining a pawn chain** — capture the pawn that supports an advanced enemy pawn or outpost.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[I see a threat that does not work] --> B[Which enemy piece stops it]
  B --> C[Is that the only defender]
  C -->|No| D[Look for a different idea]
  C -->|Yes| E[Can I capture the defender]
  E -->|Yes| F[Calculate capture then execute threat]
  E -->|No| G[Can I chase or deflect it]
  G -->|Yes| F
  G -->|No| D
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Capture the knight, then mate

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . n . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . . . R . B K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to play and win.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/5n2/8/8/8/6PP/4R1BK w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/5n2/8/8/8/6PP/4R1BK_w_-_-_0_1

The knight on f6 is the **only** guard of e8. White's bishop on g1 hits f6 nothing — instead White uses the rook. Wait: the cleanest win is **1.Bxf6** is not legal (g1-bishop doesn't reach f6). Correct solution: White cannot capture f6 here, so this diagram is identical to Diagram 2's lesson. **Use Example 2 below for the executable capture.** Treat Example 1 as the recognition drill: the defender is the f6-knight, and you must create a way to remove it (e.g. by maneuvering a piece to attack f6) before Re8 works.

### Example 2 — Trade off the back-rank guard

```
8 . . . . r . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to play and mate in two.*
**FEN:** 4r1k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/4r1k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1

The black rook on e8 is the sole defender of the e-file and the back rank. **1.Rxe8+** removes it; Black must recapture **1...Rxe8**? — but Black has no other rook, so the king is forced: **1...Kf8/Kh8 are illegal (rook is pinned to nothing; king cannot interpose)**. After **1.Rxe8+ Kf8** is impossible because e8 is occupied by White's rook giving check from e8; the king must move to a square off the back rank, but f7/g7/h7 are blocked by pawns — wait, the king can play to f8? No, e8 rook checks along the 8th and the f8 square is attacked. The king has no legal move, and nothing can capture the e8-rook. Therefore **1.Rxe8#** is mate immediately: the defender was captured *with check*, and removing it was the mate itself.

## Common mistakes

- **Striking before removing.** Playing the mating/winning move while the guard is still there (e.g. 1.Re8?? when a knight covers e8) just hangs your piece.
- **Misidentifying the defender.** Two pieces may guard the square; removing only one changes nothing.
- **Ignoring the recapture.** After you capture the guard, check whether the recapturing piece *also* defends the key square.
- **Forgetting in-between checks.** The defender may give check or create a counter-threat as it's removed.
- **Not looking for a chase.** When you can't capture the guard, a pawn push or attack that forces it to move is often available.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** filter for `attraction`, `deflection`, and `defensiveMove`; do **30 puzzles/day** focused on "what is defending the key square?" before calculating.
- **Back-rank drills:** set up the Diagram 1 / Example 2 patterns and play them out vs. an engine, **10 reps**, until Rxf8+ / Rxe8# is automatic.
- **Books:** *Chess: 5334 Problems, Combinations and Games* (Polgar) — mate-in-two section; *The Art of Attack in Chess* (Vukovic) for the focal-point/defender chapters.
- **Annotation habit:** in your own games, after every missed tactic, write down "the defender was ___." Build a list; patterns repeat.
- **Speed reps:** 5-minute timed sets of 10 "remove the guard" puzzles to wire in pattern recognition under clock pressure.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5RPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Find the forced mate.
2. **FEN:** 4r1k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Mate in one by removing the defender.
3. **FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Is Rd8 mate? Why or why not, and what guards the square?
4. **FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/6PP/2R3K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. The c1-rook wants the 8th rank; what stops Rc8 and how would you remove it?

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Rxf8+ Rxf8 2.Re8#.** The f8-rook is the only guard of the back-rank entry; capture it, then the second rook mates on e8. The king is smothered by f7-g7-h7 pawns.
2. **1.Rxe8#.** The e8-rook is the sole defender; capturing it *is* the mate — the rook checks along the 8th rank from e8, the king is boxed in by its pawns, and nothing can recapture.
3. **Yes, 1.Rd8#.** There is no black defender on the 8th rank at all; the king on g8 is smothered by f7-g7-h7, so Rd8 is immediate mate. No removal needed — the recognition point is that *no* defender exists.
4. **The black rook on f8 guards the 8th rank**, so 1.Rc8?? Rxc8 fails. You must first remove or deflect the f8-rook — e.g. exchange a pair of rooks onto the back rank or chase it — before Rc8 mates. With only the c1-rook, capture isn't available, so this is a chase/deflection setup, not an immediate win.

</details>

## Related

- [Deflection](deflection.md) — [[deflection]]
- [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md) — [[back-rank-mate]]
- [Overloaded pieces](overloaded-pieces.md) — [[overloaded-piece]]
- [Pins](pins.md) — [[pin]]
- [Decoy and attraction](decoy.md) — [[decoy]]

content.content:
1	# Removing the Defender
2	
3	> Eliminating or driving away the piece that guards a key square, a key piece, or a mating square — so the threat behind it finally lands.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
6	**Prerequisites:** [Back-Rank Mate](back-rank-mate.md), [Pins](pins.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	Most defensive setups rest on a single overworked piece: one knight that guards the mating square, one rook that holds the back rank, one bishop that stops the breakthrough. The blow you want to deliver is already "there" — it is simply blocked by one defender doing one job.
11	
12	**Removing the Defender** is the systematic answer. Instead of fighting the threat indirectly, you attack the *defender itself* and eliminate it in one of three ways:
13	
14	1. **Capture it** — even sacrificing material, if the threat it was guarding is worth more.
15	2. **Chase it** — attack it with a pawn or lesser piece so it must abandon its post.
16	3. **Pin or trade it** — make it unable to perform its defensive duty (overlaps with the pin and deflection).
17	
18	Once the guard is gone, the threat executes on the now-undefended square or piece.
19	
20	## Why it matters
21	
22	At the board you are always asking "what stops my idea?" Nine times out of ten the answer is *one piece*. Training yourself to name that piece — then to ask "can I take it, chase it, or pin it?" — converts vague attacking energy into concrete forcing lines. It is the connective tissue between *seeing* a mating pattern and actually *delivering* it. Removing the Defender underlies most back-rank wins, smothered-mate setups, and combinations where a "protected" piece suddenly hangs.
23	
24	## Diagrams
25	
26	```
27	8 . . . . . r k .
28	7 . . . . . p p p
29	6 . . . . . . . .
30	5 . . . . . . . .
31	4 . . . . . . . .
32	3 . . . . . . . .
33	2 . . . . . . P P
34	1 . . . . R . K .
35	```
36	*The defender at work: the rook on f8 is the ONLY thing keeping White off the eighth rank.*
37	**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/6PP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
38	Side to move: **White**
39	https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/6PP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1
40	
41	Black's king is boxed in by f7-g7-h7. White would love `Re8#`, but `1.Re8 Rxe8` just trades — the f8-rook defends e8. With a single attacker you can identify the defender but not yet beat it. You need a *second* attacker, as in the next diagram.
42	
43	```
44	8 . r . . . . k .
45	7 . . . . . p p p
46	6 . . . . . . . .
47	5 . . . . . . . .
48	4 . . . . . . . .
49	3 . . . . . . . .
50	2 . . . R . . P P
51	1 . . . R . . K .
52	```
53	*Doubled rooks remove the lone defender, then mate: **1.Rd8+ Rxd8 2.Rxd8#**.*
54	**FEN:** 1r4k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R2PP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1
55	Side to move: **White**
56	https://lichess.org/analysis/1r4k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R2PP/3R2K1_w_-_-_0_1
57	
58	The b8-rook is Black's only back-rank defender. **1.Rd8+** forces **1...Rxd8** (the *only* legal reply — the king cannot reach d8 and has no luft). Now the defender is gone: **2.Rxd8#**. The back rook recaptures and mates; f7-g7-h7 seal the king. Two attackers versus one defender wins.
59	
60	## Patterns and sub-types
61	
62	- **Capture the defender (often a sacrifice):** give up material to remove the only guard of a mating square — the classic back-rank and smothered-mate prelude.
63	- **Chase the defender:** a pawn push or minor-piece attack forces the guard to move, undefending the key square.
64	- **Pin the defender:** a pinned piece "exists" but cannot defend — the guarded square is effectively unprotected (see [Pins](pins.md)).
65	- **Trade the defender:** exchange off the lone guardian, then the threat lands next move.
66	- **Guard of a piece vs. guard of a square:** removing it can win the now-hanging piece *or* deliver mate on the now-open square.
67	- **Overlap with [Deflection](deflection.md) and [Decoy](decoy.md):** deflection lures the defender away; removal eliminates it outright.
68	
69	## How to spot it
70	
71	```mermaid
72	flowchart TD
73	    A[I have a threat that is blocked] --> B[Name the one piece that stops it]
74	    B --> C{Can I capture that piece}
75	    C -->|Yes| D{Is the threat worth the cost}
76	    D -->|Yes| E[Capture then execute]
77	    D -->|No| F{Can I chase or pin it}
78	    C -->|No| F
79	    F -->|Chase| G[Hit it with a pawn or minor]
80	    F -->|Pin| H[Pin it so it cannot guard]
81	    G --> E
82	    H --> E
83	```
84	
85	## Worked examples
86	
87	### Example 1 — Capture the knight that guards the mating square
88	
89	```
90	8 . . . . . . k .
91	7 . . . . . p p p
92	6 . . . n . . . .
93	5 . . . . . . . .
94	4 . . . . . . . .
95	3 . . . . . . . .
96	2 . . . . . . P P
97	1 . . . R R . K .
98	```
99	*The knight on d6 is the only thing guarding e8. **1.Rxd6!** removes it.*
100	**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/3n4/8/8/8/6PP/3RR1K1 w - - 0 1
101	Side to move: **White**
102	https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/3n4/8/8/8/6PP/3RR1K1_w_-_-_0_1
103	
104	White wants `Re8#`, but the d6-knight covers e8. The knight is undefended, so **1.Rxd6!** wins it outright *and* removes the guard, leaving the crushing threat of **2.Re8#**. Black cannot both save a piece and create luft in one move: e.g. `1...h6 2.Re8+ Kh7 3.Rxa8` (or any back-rank harvest) leaves White a clean rook ahead. Capture the defender — here it is even free — and the rest collapses.
105	
106	### Example 2 — Chase the defender with a bishop
107	
108	```
109	8 . . . . . . k .
110	7 . . . . . p p p
111	6 . . n . . . . .
112	5 . B . . . . . .
113	4 . . . . . . . .
114	3 . . . . . . . .
115	2 . . . . . . P P
116	1 . . . R . . K .
117	```
118	*The knight on c6 guards d8; the bishop on b5 attacks it. **1.Bxc6** removes the guard.*
119	**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/2n5/1B6/8/8/6PP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1
120	Side to move: **White**
121	https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/2n5/1B6/8/8/6PP/3R2K1_w_-_-_0_1
122	
123	White's rook eyes `Rd8#`, but the c6-knight defends d8. The knight is undefended, so **1.Bxc6** captures the defender and wins a clean piece while leaving the threat of `Rd8`. Note the limit honestly: with only one rook, after `1...h6` Black makes luft, so this is "win a piece," not a forced mate. The lesson stands — *find the guard, then attack it.*
124	
125	## Common mistakes
126	
127	- **Forgetting the second attacker.** Removing the guard of a mating square does nothing if no piece remains to occupy it. Count attackers vs. the target square *first* — Diagram 1 needs two rooks, not one.
128	- **Removing the wrong guard.** A square is often defended twice; eliminate only one and the threat still fails.
129	- **Grabbing a defended piece.** Verify the target is undefended, or that the recapture costs your opponent more than you give.
130	- **Ignoring in-between checks.** The defender's owner may have a zwischenzug (check or counter-capture) that saves the day before you cash in.
131	- **Allowing luft.** A single back-rank attacker lets the king escape after `...h6/g6`; make sure the blow is forcing.
132	
133	## Train it
134	
135	- **Lichess puzzle themes:** train *Deflection*, *Sacrifice*, *Back-rank mate*, and *Attraction* — these hold the bulk of removing-the-defender motifs. Do **20 puzzles/day** for two weeks, saying out loud "the defender is ___" before each move.
136	- **Books:** *Chess: 5334 Problems, Combinations and Games* (László Polgar) — the mate-in-2 section; and *The Art of Attack in Chess* (Vuković), the chapter on destroying the defence. **10 positions/session.**
137	- **Drill:** set up Diagram 2 against an engine and deliver `1.Rd8+ Rxd8 2.Rxd8#` from memory **5 times**, then swap the defender (rook → knight → bishop) and re-find the win.
138	- **Calculation reps:** **3 forcing-move puzzles/day** where the first move must capture a defender.
139	
140	## Exercises
141	
142	1. **FEN:** `1r4k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R2PP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1` — *White to move.* Force mate.
143	2. **FEN:** `6k1/5ppp/3n4/8/8/8/6PP/3RR1K1 w - - 0 1` — *White to move.* Win material by removing the guard of e8.
144	3. **FEN:** `6k1/5ppp/2n5/1B6/8/8/6PP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1` — *White to move.* Eliminate the defender of d8.
145	4. **FEN:** `r5k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R2PP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1` — *White to move.* Same motif as #1 with the defender on a8 — force mate.
146	
147	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
148	
149	1. **1.Rd8+ Rxd8 2.Rxd8#.** `1.Rd8+` forces `1...Rxd8` (the only legal move — the king cannot reach d8 and has no escape). The defending rook is gone, so the back rook recaptures with mate on d8; f7-g7-h7 trap the king.
150	2. **1.Rxd6!** The d6-knight is undefended and is the only guard of e8. Capturing it wins a clean knight and leaves the threat `2.Re8#`; Black cannot save the piece and stop the mate in one move, so White emerges a rook up at minimum.
151	3. **1.Bxc6.** The c6-knight (the sole guard of d8) is undefended. White wins it for free and keeps the `Rd8` threat alive. With a single rook this is "win a piece," not forced mate — note that `1...h6` would otherwise make luft.
152	4. **1.Rd8+ Rxd8 2.Rxd8#.** Identical idea to #1: the a8-rook is the lone back-rank defender; the check forces `...Rxd8`, and the second rook mates on d8.
153	
154	</details>
155	
156	## Related
157	
158	- [Deflection](deflection.md) — luring the defender away rather than capturing it.
159	- [Decoy](decoy.md) — dragging a piece onto a fatal square.
160	- [Back-Rank Mate](back-rank-mate.md) — the threat most often unlocked by removing a defender.
161	- [Pins](pins.md) — a pinned defender cannot defend.
162	- [Overloading](overloading.md) — one piece saddled with too many duties.
163	
164	Concept tags: [[removing-the-defender]] [[deflection]] [[back-rank-mate]] [[overloading]] [[pin]]
165

content.text: Now let me verify each ASCII board exactly matches its FEN with a quick programmatic check, and get the word count.

input.command:
cd /c/GrandMaster/03-Tactics && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re

text=open('removing-the-defender.md',encoding='utf-8').read()

# word count
words=len(re.findall(r"\S+", text))
print("WORDS:",words)

# Extract diagram blocks (8 rank lines) and following FEN
blocks=re.findall(r"```\n(8 .*?\n.*?\n.*?\n.*?\n.*?\n.*?\n.*?\n1 .*?)\n  a b c d e f g h\n```\s*\n\*.*?\*\n\*\*FEN:\*\* (\S+)", text, re.S)

def fen_to_board(fen):
    rows=fen.split('/')
    out=[]
    for r in rows:
        line=[]
        for ch in r:
            if ch.isdigit():
                line+=['.']*int(ch)
            else:
                line.append(ch)
        out.append(line)
    return out

def ascii_to_board(block):
    out=[]
    for ln in block.split('\n'):
        parts=ln.split(' ')
        # parts[0] is rank digit
        cells=parts[1:9]
        out.append(cells)
    return out

print("Diagrams found:",len(blocks))
for i,(block,fen) in enumerate(blocks,1):
    fenpos=fen.split()[0]
    fb=fen_to_board(fenpos)
    ab=ascii_to_board(block)
    ok = fb==ab
    print(f"Diagram {i}: FEN={fen}  MATCH={ok}")
    if not ok:
        for r in range(8):
            if fb[r]!=ab[r]:
                print("  rank",8-r,"fen",fb[r],"ascii",ab[r])
EOF
